name: X-RAY - cursor fluid-trail unmasks a hidden scene layer beneath each work
ref: Codrops 'Building a Dual-Scene Fluid X-Ray Reveal Effect in Three.js' (March 2026 tympanus.net/codrops/2026/03/23/building-a-dual-scene-fluid-x-ray-reveal-effect-in-three-js); Lusion creative studio WebGL post-processing experiments (lusion.co)
palette: #050a12 (abyss) + #00f0c8 (bioluminescent teal for the revealed layer); outer scene is desaturated near-black, inner scene is full-color
brief: Each gallery card renders two overlapping states: an outer desaturated version and a hidden full-color or skeleton layer. As the cursor moves, a ping-pong fluid simulation accumulates the trail; the resulting organic mask blends between the two states wherever fluid is active. When the cursor stops, fluid diffuses and restores the outer state over about 1 second. Distinct from spotlight-noir (geometric radial falloff, no fluid physics) and liquid-distortion (morphs between different works, not dual states of one work). The fluid mask is active at scroll=0 as soon as the cursor enters. Mobile: tap-and-hold triggers a radial fluid burst centered on the touch point, auto-fading after 1.5 s. WebGL note: avoid GLSL ES 1.0 dynamic-indexed local arrays since ANGLE returns 0; use uniform arrays indexed by a loop variable only; confirm canvas paints before the render loop starts; use preserveDrawingBuffer if screenshot verification is needed. Guardrail: render at DPR 1; ping-pong FBO at half resolution; mobile falls back to a CSS clip-path radial reveal on tap. Implement with three.js via importmap for the fluid simulation; all other UI with native CSS/JS, NO GSAP or other external JS libraries.

name: CORRIDOR - first-person walkable 3D corridor, hover paints sketches to color
ref: Codrops 'Sketching the Impossible: A 3D Portfolio Built Without a Single 3D Model' (June 2026 tympanus.net/codrops/2026/06/11/sketching-the-impossible-a-3d-portfolio-built-without-a-single-3d-model/); GSAP Site of the Day 2026 showcase featuring first-person WebGL portfolio environments (gsap.com/showcase)
palette: #f5f0e6 (sketchbook paper) + #1a1a1a (ink line); color appears only on hover-painted zones, no global accent hue
brief: The entire site is a first-person walkable 3D corridor built from flat textured planes (no 3D models). Scrolling moves the camera forward and backward through an infinite chunked corridor; mouse tilt adds parallax camera lean. Doorways branch off at intervals and each one, when approached, expands to reveal a work. Signature: hovering any surface triggers a GLSL paint-fill shader that floods the ink sketch with color - color equals clickable. At scroll=0 the corridor is already visible in first-person perspective and the paint effect fires on first mouse-enter, demonstrating both mechanics within seconds. Distinct from space (WebGL fly-through of framed planes in Z-space with no corridor walls or paint reveal), orbit (hero model + bento, not walkable), and infinite-canvas (2D pan/zoom board, no first-person perspective). Mobile: gyroscope drives the tilt; tap-and-hold triggers the paint fill. WebGL note: avoid GLSL ES 1.0 dynamic-indexed local arrays since ANGLE returns 0; use sampler2D texture LUTs for the paint mask; confirm canvas paints before the render loop starts; use preserveDrawingBuffer if needed. Guardrail: corridor chunks load lazily via IntersectionObserver on sentinel divs; render at DPR 1; mobile disables the paint shader and uses a CSS tint overlay instead. Implement with three.js via importmap; all UI chrome with native CSS/JS, NO GSAP or other external JS libraries.

name: MORPH NAV - CSS View Transitions shared-element click-through gallery
ref: Cyd Stumpel Portfolio (Awwwards SOTD 2025, cydstumpel.nl; Awwwards jury cited CSS View Transitions and Scroll Driven Animations); CSS View Transitions API Baseline Newly Available cross-browser Oct 2025 (Chrome 111+, Firefox 133+, Safari 18+; developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/View_Transition_API)
palette: #f5f1eb (off-white index) + #1a3020 (deep forest green detail); the morph makes the color shift feel like turning a physical card over
brief: At scroll=0 the full index grid of thumbnail cards is visible - click any card and the View Transitions API morphs that thumbnail's exact DOM node (image, size, position) directly into the full-bleed detail hero on the next route, with no page-reload feel; back-navigation reverses the morph. The signature is that navigation itself is the spectacle: the same image physically reshapes and flies across two completely different page compositions. Distinct from every existing template which is about within-page scroll or cursor interaction; this paradigm is about inter-state navigation choreography where one DOM element persists visually across route boundaries. Distinct from CSS fold/unfold (within-page reveal) and from OS boot (a loading ceremony). The index grid is visible at scroll=0 ready to morph on first click. Mobile: works identically; touch tap triggers the shared-element morph. Guardrail: one startViewTransition() call plus view-transition-name tags; progressive enhancement - degrades to instant navigation without transitions on unsupported browsers; no animation library needed; no WebGL. Implement with native CSS/JS View Transitions API, NO GSAP or other external JS libraries.

name: CUBE REEL - CSS 3D cube dual-viewport synchronized gallery
ref: Codrops 'Building a Scroll-Driven 3D Cube Gallery' (May 2026 tympanus.net/codrops/2026/05/26/building-a-scroll-driven-3d-cube-gallery-in-webflow-with-gsap); 3D cube scroll-driven interaction pattern cited in Codrops 2025 Year in Review (tympanus.net/codrops/2025/12/29/2025-a-very-special-year-in-review/)
palette: #111111 (matte black) + #e8e0d4 (raw linen); cube faces show color photography, the two neutrals keep geometry legible without competing
brief: At scroll=0 a split composition is live: a full-bleed background image fills the page while a floating CSS 3D cube (preserve-3d) positioned to one side shows the same image on its front face. As the user scrolls, the background crossfades to the next work and simultaneously the cube rotates on multiple axes to display the matching face, also translating along a short compound path (rotation plus lateral drift). Type stacks animate word-by-word via CSS clip-path mask-clipping. The cube is a synchronized second-screen for the gallery content, not a decoration. Distinct from ring-carousel (passive loop, no background sync) and coverflow-3d (horizontal flat cards on Y-axis only, no background crossfade). The split composition with live cube is visible at scroll=0 immediately. Mobile: cube scales down to a corner pip; rotation range is reduced to X-axis only for legibility. Guardrail: all 3D is CSS transform-style: preserve-3d with CSS perspective; no WebGL; requestAnimationFrame only for the crossfade lerp; will-change: transform on the cube face elements. Implement with native CSS/JS, NO GSAP or other external JS libraries.

name: IRIS - sticky grid cinematic expansion from compressed to full-bleed
ref: Codrops 'Sticky Grid Scroll: Building a Scroll-Driven Animated Grid' (March 2026 tympanus.net/codrops/2026/03/02/sticky-grid-scroll-building-a-scroll-driven-animated-grid/); Dropbox Brand site (CSSDA WOTY 2025 nominee, staggered-grid reflow cited by jury)
palette: #1e2530 (deep slate) + #d4c9b8 (warm sand); grid images provide all color, container transitions from dark during the iris phase to sand on the settled final state
brief: At scroll=0 a centered headline sits in front of a 3-column image grid whose columns are compressed tight behind the headline. Scrolling through a long sticky section (about 400vh) triggers three sequential phases purely via one scroll-progress CSS custom property: columns animate inward staggered from alternating vertical directions to fill the screen (Phase 1); the grid then scales outward and side columns push apart as if the camera zooms through the center (Phase 2); the headline repositions and a call-to-action fades in (Phase 3). The scroll never moves the page up during this journey. After completion normal scroll resumes into a detail panel. Distinct from morphing-grid (grid layout reflows between named formations) and sticky-grid-unfold (cells unlock in a spiral sequence). The compressed grid behind the headline is visible at scroll=0, signaling the imminent bloom. Mobile: 2-column grid; Phase 2 scale range is halved for smaller screens. Guardrail: all animation is driven by a single scroll listener mapping progress to CSS custom properties; CSS Grid handles layout; no canvas; no WebGL; reduced-motion collapses all three phases to an instant opacity reveal. Implement with native CSS/JS, NO GSAP or other external JS libraries.

name: ARC FLOW - thumbnail cards follow authored bezier arcs into final composition
ref: Codrops Creative Hub demo archive featuring MotionPath card choreography patterns (tympanus.net/codrops/hub/all/codrops/ June 2026); Buttermax (CSSDA WOTY 2026 score 9.06, buttermax.net; jury cited innovative UI/UX navigation choreography)
palette: #fafafa (chalk white) + #0d1b2a (ink navy); motion trail lines in translucent navy 20% opacity; card images provide all color
brief: At scroll=0 all project thumbnail cards are in a stacked rest position at center screen, the only signal that something is about to move. The first scroll tick fans each card out along its own pre-authored cubic bezier SVG path: one arcs left and settles top-left, another spirals in from off-screen bottom-right, a third drops from above, until all lock into a final asymmetric composed layout. Faint trail lines persist briefly along each arc. Clicking any card reverses it along its path while the detail view enters from a complementary arc. Distinct from draggable-scatter (physics simulation, random non-deterministic paths) and elastic-ribbon (cursor-driven drag and snap). Paths are deterministic authored bezier curves; the composition is the ending frame of a small choreographed film. Signature: stacked rest state at scroll=0 signals imminent fan-out. Mobile: arc radii are scaled to 60% of desktop values and the final layout switches to a 2-column grid. Guardrail: paths are SVG path strings consumed by a native requestAnimationFrame lerp along the path using SVGPathElement.getPointAtLength(); no external MotionPath library required; no WebGL; reduced-motion collapses to a simultaneous opacity fade into the final layout. If any SVG path fill or stroke is used decoratively, it must be a hex or rgb() value not a CSS var() in a presentation attribute. Implement with native CSS/JS, NO GSAP or other external JS libraries.

name: LETTERBOX - giant text acts as a clipping mask over full-bleed imagery
ref: CSS Masking Level 2 text clip-mask technique widely featured on Awwwards SOTD and Codrops 2025-2026 (tympanus.net/codrops, multiple text-clip-path articles); Obys Agency 2026 kinetic branding portfolio (obys.agency, Awwwards SOTD multiple 2025-2026) featuring text-as-window compositions
palette: #0a0a0a (background void) + #f5f2ed (bone text fill fallback); imagery shows only through the letterforms, no exterior color from the artwork
brief: At scroll=0 a single oversized word or logotype fills the viewport and acts as a CSS background-clip: text window into a full-bleed hero image or video playing behind it; the background outside the letterforms is pure void so only the image-through-text reads. Scrolling advances through a sequence of words/titles each with its own image behind the clip. The second level: hovering a letterform shifts the background-position of only that letter's backing image (letter-level parallax), treating each glyph as an independent viewport tile. Distinct from morph (variable font weight animation with media behind/inside SVG cutout shapes) and variable-font-pressure (weight-axis index, no clipping). The background-clip text effect is a different rendering paradigm: the entire image lives inside the letterform outlines with no SVG, no WebGL, pure CSS. At scroll=0 the first word with its image interior is immediately visible. Mobile: pointer parallax falls back to a scroll-based background-position shift. Guardrail: background-clip: text is applied on the headline element with -webkit- prefix for Safari; the backing image or video is a positioned ::before pseudo-element so the text color can remain transparent; no WebGL; no canvas; no SVG required for the clip. If inline SVG is used for any decorative element, fill and stroke must be hex or rgb() values not CSS var() in presentation attributes. Implement with native CSS/JS, NO GSAP or other external JS libraries.

name: PURE SCROLL - zero-JS CSS scroll-timeline driven gallery reveal
ref: CSS Scroll-Driven Animations spec (Baseline 2024, Chrome 115+ / Safari 18+ / Firefox 133+; developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/animation-timeline/scroll); Codrops 'Creative Uses of Scroll-Driven Animations' (April 2026 tympanus.net/codrops/2026/04/03/creative-uses-of-scroll-driven-animations/) citing zero-JS portfolios as a 2026 frontier
palette: #f8f5f0 (warm paper) + #111111 (ink); each work supplies its own color, the gallery chrome stays ink-on-paper
brief: The entire gallery runs without a single line of JavaScript: works slide, rotate, scale, and colorize purely via animation-timeline: scroll(root) and view() bound to @keyframes through @property animated custom properties. Each card has a distinct entrance choreography (one fades, one slides from left, one rotates from below) coded in plain CSS, demonstrating the scroll-timeline + view-timeline API surface. A CSS-only reading-progress bar using animation-timeline: scroll() runs in the fixed header. The zero-JS constraint is the brand claim - a header badge reads '0 kB JS'. Distinct from scroll-velocity-mood (JS rAF loop driving CSS vars), blind-mask-wipe (JS IntersectionObserver), and all other existing templates that rely on JavaScript for animation. The first card is mid-entrance at scroll=0, proving the CSS animation is live on load. Mobile: identical - no JS dependency means no mobile-performance penalty. Guardrail: use @supports (animation-timeline: scroll()) to gate the enhanced experience; unsupported browsers receive a static opacity:1 fallback via the cascade; no WebGL; no canvas. If inline SVG is used for any badge or divider, fill and stroke must be hex values not CSS var() in presentation attributes. Implement with pure CSS and HTML, absolutely NO JavaScript.

name: LENTICULAR - CSS-only two-image lenticular print flip on cursor X
ref: Physical lenticular collectible-card revival trend featured across Awwwards SOTD nominees 2026 (awwwards.com); Codrops 'Recreating Lenticular Effects with CSS Perspective' (February 2026 tympanus.net/codrops/2026/02/06/recreating-lenticular-effects-with-css-perspective/) detailing rotateY + backface-visibility technique
palette: #1c1c1e (deep graphite card backing) + #f0ede8 (bone reverse face); imagery dominates both faces, chrome stays neutral
brief: Each card is a CSS preserve-3d element with two faces: the front face shows the finished work, the back face shows the prompt or raw draft. As the cursor moves horizontally across a card the card rotates on the Y-axis (range -20deg to +20deg) using cursor X normalized to the card bounds via a CSS custom property updated by one pointer-move listener per card. Crossing zero rotateY flips to the back face via backface-visibility. The parallax flip itself is the interaction - no scroll, no click required. At scroll=0 the card grid is fully visible with cards slightly pre-tilted to signal the lenticular mechanic. Distinct from retro-futurism-holo (hue-rotating conic gradient foil overlay, no two-face physical flip) and coverflow-3d (rotating a stack of flat cards in a carousel, not per-card face reveal). Mobile: device orientation beta axis drives the tilt in place of cursor X; a tap locks a card face-forward and shows a detail overlay. Guardrail: pure CSS transform-style: preserve-3d; backface-visibility: hidden on each face; one passive pointermove listener per card updating a CSS custom property; no WebGL; no canvas; no animation library. If any decorative SVG on the card back uses fill or stroke it must be a hex value not a CSS var() in a presentation attribute. Implement with native CSS/JS, NO GSAP or other external JS libraries.

name: CURVED TYPE - expressive SVG text-on-path festival poster with scroll reveal
ref: Codrops 'SVG Text on a Path: Advanced Techniques' (March 2026 tympanus.net/codrops/2026/03/16/svg-text-on-a-path-advanced-techniques/) covering stroke-dashoffset scroll-draw and textPath spacing; music festival / event poster category on Awwwards 2026 (awwwards.com/sites/tag/poster) featuring expressive curved-type hero compositions
palette: #0a0e14 (night ink) + #f5d94e (electric yellow text) + #e03c2c (vermilion accent fill); palette is a festival-print primary triad, no mid-tones
brief: Works are each a full-bleed poster where expressive handwritten-style text flows along organic SVG curves framing the image - title arcs across the top, credits spiral around the edge, a URL curves beneath. On scroll-in each text path draws itself via stroke-dashoffset animation timed to the IntersectionObserver entry. Hovering a path highlights it in the vermilion accent. Distinct from blueprint-svg (precise engineering hairlines dimensioning technical specs) and manifesto-essay (reading-column prose with pull quotes) - this is expressive decorative lettering on organic curves as the primary visual art, not a functional schematic or readable body text. At scroll=0 the first poster is already partially drawn mid-arc, demonstrating the stroke-draw effect without scrolling. Mobile: paths scale proportionally via SVG viewBox; reduced-motion delivers paths fully drawn from the start, no stroke animation. Guardrail: all text is inline SVG using textPath referencing a path element by xlink:href or href; fill on text elements must be a hex or rgb() value not a CSS var() in SVG presentation attributes since var() does not resolve there; stroke-dasharray and stroke-dashoffset are set as inline SVG attributes not CSS for maximum browser compatibility; no WebGL; no canvas. Implement with native CSS/JS + inline SVG, NO GSAP or other external JS libraries.

name: BISECT - live cursor-bisected dual composition, two simultaneous worlds
ref: Resn studio interactive dual-composition experiments (resn.co.nz, Awwwards Agency of the Year multiple times; known for cursor-split viewport compositions); Codrops 'Cursor-Driven Split-Screen Compositions' (January 2026 tympanus.net/codrops/2026/01/20/cursor-driven-split-screen-compositions/) covering clip-path polygon technique for live cursor bisection
palette: #0a0a0a (night side) + #f4f0e8 (day side); the bisect line itself is the only edge, no border or seam glyph
brief: The viewport is permanently split at the cursor X position into two simultaneous independent compositions (e.g., studio process on the left, finished work on the right; past on the left, present on the right). A clip-path polygon updated by one pointermove listener keeps the left composition clipped to the cursor X and the right composition clipped from cursor X to right edge - both composited live at 60fps. Type on each side mirrors in the opposite color so it is always legible regardless of split position. At scroll=0 the split is live at 50/50 by default, both compositions already full of content. Moving the cursor left or right instantly reveals more or less of each world. Distinct from split-diptych (sticky RAW/REFINED seam that auto-sweeps on scroll and the visitor drags a single image boundary) - bisect has two entirely different scene compositions on each side, not two states of one image. Mobile: a horizontal touch-drag bisects the two compositions top/bottom instead of left/right. Guardrail: two overlapping full-viewport layers, each clipped by a CSS clip-path: polygon() updated via a CSS custom property on pointermove; no canvas needed; no WebGL; one rAF batches the clip-path update per frame. Implement with native CSS/JS, NO GSAP or other external JS libraries.

name: SUBSTRATE - live procedural Canvas 2D noise painted in a Worker behind static cards
ref: OffscreenCanvas + Worker pattern covered in Codrops 'Offscreen Canvas and Web Workers for Smooth Generative Backgrounds' (May 2026 tympanus.net/codrops/2026/05/14/offscreen-canvas-web-workers-smooth-generative-backgrounds/); generative-substrate gallery aesthetic on Godly.website 2026 collection (godly.website) where bespoke procedural textures replace photography as the gallery floor
palette: #0c0c0c (void base) + emergent palette from the noise (low-saturation ochre #8a6a3a / slate #4a5a6a / bone #d8ccbc); work cards sit above in full color
brief: The full-viewport background is a live procedural Canvas 2D substrate painted entirely inside an OffscreenCanvas transferred to a Worker thread so the main thread stays free for card interactions. The substrate uses arithmetic value-noise with multiple octaves to produce a slowly evolving grain-and-cloud texture that shifts hue toward the dominant color of whichever work card is currently in focus. Work cards float above on a transparent layer with subtle drop shadows. Hovering a card accelerates the noise toward that card's palette; releasing lets it drift back. The generative substrate is visible at scroll=0, already animated, before any card interaction. Distinct from tech-organic-mesh (WebGL GLSL fragment shader, not Canvas 2D; no Worker) and dithered-lo-fi (1-bit Bayer dither WebGL shader applied to the images themselves, not a background substrate). Mobile: OffscreenCanvas is supported on iOS 17+ and Android Chrome; reduced-motion stops the time-tick so the substrate is static but still renders. Guardrail: OffscreenCanvas.transferControlToOffscreen() called once; Worker posts a single Float32Array noise buffer per frame and the canvas draws it in one putImageData call; DPR capped at 1 for perf; no WebGL; no CSS animation on the canvas element. If any card overlay uses inline SVG for decorative borders, fill and stroke must be hex values not CSS var() in presentation attributes. Implement with native JS + OffscreenCanvas Worker, NO GSAP or other external JS libraries.
